era_context: The late 11th century CE was a period of significant transition and conflict in medieval Europe and the broader world. The Investiture Controversy dominated Western European ecclesiastical politics. The Byzantine Empire under Alexios I Komnenos was recovering from decades of decline and seeking Western military assistance. The Seljuk Turks had firmly established themselves in Anatolia and the Levant. The Song Dynasty in China was at the height of its economic and cultural power. This was a pivotal moment in medieval history, with major geopolitical shifts and cultural developments occurring simultaneously across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited written records)

events.title: Deaths of major Song Dynasty reformers Wang Anshi and Sima Guang
events.description: In 1086, two of the most influential figures in Song Dynasty history died: Wang Anshi (1021–1086), the great reformer and statesman, and Sima Guang (1019–1086), the historian and conservative statesman. Wang Anshi had implemented sweeping administrative and economic reforms during his tenure as chief minister, including the New Policies (Xin Fa) that aimed to strengthen the state and increase revenue. Sima Guang had opposed Wang's reforms and represented the conservative faction. Their deaths marked the end of an era of intense political conflict over the direction of Song policy. The deaths of these two giants of Song politics left the court in a state of transition regarding administrative direction and policy. Emperor Zhezong would now establish his own authority and direction for the empire.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government) by Sima Guang

events.certainty_note: The deaths of Wang Anshi and Sima Guang in 1086 are well-documented in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Domesday Book completed in England under William the Conqueror
events.description: The Domesday Book, a comprehensive survey of English lands and resources commissioned by William the Conqueror, was completed in 1086. This remarkable administrative document recorded the ownership, value, and resources of virtually every piece of land in England, creating an unprecedented inventory of the kingdom's wealth and population. The Domesday Book was both a practical administrative tool for taxation and a symbol of Norman power and control over England. It represents one of the most sophisticated administrative achievements of the medieval period and provides modern historians with invaluable information about 11th-century England. The completion of the Domesday Book demonstrated the Norman ability to impose centralized administrative control over a conquered kingdom.
key_figures: William I, the Conqueror (commissioned the survey)
events.certainty_note: The Domesday Book's completion in 1086 is well-documented in contemporary sources and the document itself survives.

events.title: Battle of Sagrajas (Zallaqa): Christian Iberian forces defeated by Almoravid Berbers
events.description: In 1086, Christian Iberian forces under Alfonso VI of Castile were defeated by the Almoravid Berber dynasty from North Africa at the Battle of Sagrajas (also known as Zallaqa). The Almoravids, a puritanical Islamic reform movement from the Sahara, had been invited to Iberia by Muslim emirs to counter Christian expansion. The Almoravid victory halted Christian reconquest efforts and demonstrated the military power of the North African Islamic movement. However, the Almoravids would eventually establish their own rule in Iberia, displacing the local Muslim emirs. The battle represented a major turning point in the Reconquista, temporarily reversing Christian momentum and introducing a new Islamic power into the peninsula.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Sagrajas in 1086 is well-documented in both Islamic and Christian sources.

events.title: Khitan Liao Dynasty maintains stability and cultural development in northern China
events.description: The Khitan Liao Dynasty (907–1125), centered in Manchuria and northern China, maintained its independent state and cultural development in 1086. The Liao were a sophisticated nomadic-sedentary hybrid state with advanced administrative systems, urban centers, and cultural achievements. The Liao maintained the balance of power with the Song Dynasty through the treaty system established in 1005. The Liao's stability and cultural development paralleled that of the Song Dynasty, with both states representing sophisticated medieval civilizations. The Liao would continue to exist until 1125, when they were conquered by the Jurchen Jin Dynasty.
key_figures: Liao emperors of the late 11th century

sources.name: Liao Shi (History of the Liao Dynasty)
Song Shi references to Liao relations

events.certainty_note: The Liao Dynasty's existence and general political situation in 1086 are well-documented, though specific events for this particular year are less detailed.
cross_references: 1125-001 (Fall of Liao Dynasty to Jurchen Jin)

events.title: Seljuk Sultanate under Malik Shah I continues expansion and consolidation
events.description: The Seljuk Empire under Malik Shah I (r. 1072–1092) was at the height of its power and territorial extent in 1086. Malik Shah had expanded Seljuk control over Persia, Mesopotamia, Syria, and Anatolia, creating a vast Islamic empire. The Seljuks were consolidating administrative control, developing urban centers, and patronizing Islamic culture and learning. The Seljuk Empire represented a major Islamic power that dominated the eastern Mediterranean and Middle East. However, the empire was beginning to show signs of internal fragmentation, with various atabegs (military governors) establishing semi-independent rule in different regions. The death of Malik Shah in 1092 would accelerate this fragmentation.
events.certainty_note: Seljuk political and military developments in the late 11th century are well-documented in Islamic sources.
cross_references: 1092-001 (Death of Malik Shah I and Nizam al-Mulk)

disconfirming_evidence: The entry on the Battle of Sagrajas is accurate regarding the date (1086) and the general outcome (Almoravid victory). However, some sources place the battle in 1086 while others use different dating systems or refer to it by different names (Zallaqa, Sagrajas, Talavera). The significance of the battle in halting Christian reconquest is sometimes overstated; Christian expansion resumed within a few decades. The entry on the Domesday Book is accurate regarding its completion in 1086 and its significance as an administrative document. However, the characterization of it as representing 'unprecedented' administrative sophistication may be somewhat overstated; earlier Islamic and Byzantine states had conducted similar surveys.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1086 is moderately rich for Western Europe (Domesday Book, Norman England, Iberian warfare), East Asia (Song and Liao dynasties), and the Islamic world (Seljuk Empire, Almoravid expansion), but sparse for other regions. The sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military history. The year 1086 marks several significant transitions: the completion of the Domesday Book in England, the deaths of major Song reformers in China, and the Almoravid intervention in Iberia. These events set the stage for major developments in the following decades. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of northern Europe are virtually absent from the surviving written record for this year. The period is characterized by the consolidation of major medieval states and the beginning of significant geopolitical shifts that would define the late 11th and 12th centuries.

graph_edges.note: Deaths of Wang Anshi and Sima Guang led to Emperor Zhezong's consolidation of power
Domesday Book completed under William the Conqueror; he died the following year
Almoravid intervention in Iberia occurred during the same period as First Crusade preparations
Seljuk consolidation under Malik Shah led to fragmentation after his death in 1092
